notable.notable_blurb: Agent and co-owner of Granite Mills, Talcottville

parents.father_research_notes: Father: Gurden Talcott — Talcott book number not yet identified. NOT a son of T01052 Samuel S. Talcott as initially speculated. Gurden Talcott needs to be placed in the Talcott family tree. NOTE: Tankerhoosen site accidentally pasted Samuel S.'s bio details into this entry (birth date 1793, death 1882, etc. all belong to T01052, not to Smith Stephen).

institutions.institution_blurb: Active agent of Granite Mills c.1864-1881; co-owner with Samuel S. and Anson F. Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Methodist philanthropist who managed the Granite Mills
narrative_blocks.body: Smith Stephen Talcott was the active manager of the Granite Mills from the 1860s onward. His obituary noted he "gave generously of his time and money to the Methodist church" and that health issues had brought him to Colorado annually, where he had invested successfully in mines and banking. He was 40 years younger than his co-owner Samuel S.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty years younger than his co-owner
narrative_blocks.body: Smith Stephen ran the Granite Mills beside Samuel S. Talcott — a kinsman from a different branch born forty years before him, whose birth date the Tankerhoosen records still wrongly assign to Smith. Failing health sent him to Colorado each year, where he invested successfully in mines and banking.

Gurden Talcott needs to be placed in the Talcott family tree. institutions.institution_blurb: Active agent of Granite Mills c.1864-1881; co-owner with Samuel S. and Anson F. Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Methodist philanthropist who managed the Granite Mills
narrative_blocks.body: Smith Stephen Talcott was the active manager of the Granite Mills from the 1860s onward. His obituary noted he "gave generously of his time and money to the Methodist church" and that health issues had brought him to Colorado annually, where he had invested successfully in mines and banking. He was 40 years younger than his co-owner Samuel S.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty years younger than his co-owner
narrative_blocks.body: Smith Stephen ran the Granite Mills beside Samuel S. Talcott — a kinsman from a different branch born forty years before him, whose birth date the Tankerhoosen records still wrongly assign to Smith. Failing health sent him to Colorado each year, where he invested successfully in mines and banking.

Gurdon Talcott fathered ten children in Bolton CT before his death in 1851. One son, Hiram W., was killed by Indians in California in April 1863 — during the height of the Civil War and the peak of western violence. Another son, Anson F., became co-owner of the Granite Mills in Talcottville with his distant cousin Samuel S.

parents.father_research_notes: From Columbia CT. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

INST080.name: Granite Mills

location.address: Just above Kelly Road, on the Hockanum River, Talcottville

INST080.notes:
Paper mill on the Hockanum River just above Kelly Road, Talcottville (Vernon) CT. GPS: 41.83210° N, 72.49648° W. Founded by T01052 Samuel Smith Talcott (1793-1882) as a linseed oil mill c.1838; converted to paper manufacturing after an 1863 fire. Manufactured albums, binders, boards, manilla and press papers — recycling old paper purchased in bales from New York, Boston, and Philadelphia. Destroyed by fire four times (1863, 1870, 1881, 1912) and rebuilt each time until the final 1912 fire. The 1881 fire article names owners as "Smith S. and Anson F. Talcott." Smith Stephen Talcott (1834-1888) — likely a son of T01052 Samuel — served as active agent from c.1864; he and T01052 ran the mill in parallel ("particularly confusing are the roles of the two S. S. Talcotts"). The Talcott Brothers (Charles Denison and Horace Wells) purchased the mill site in December 1912 primarily to acquire the water privilege. Despite the Talcott name, the Granite Mills Talcotts were from a different branch than the Talcottville mill owners. Site today: land between Tri-City Plaza and Hockanum River near Kelly Road; race exit from foundation preserved. Royal Talcott Journal, Nov 26, 1877: "young Nickols... got his foot caught in the belt" — earliest direct eyewitness account in the Royal Talcott Journal. SOURCE: Tankerhoosen Valley History, "Granite Mills (Circa 1850-1912)."
[former/alt detail] Also known as: Granite Paper Mills, Talcottville Paper Mill
